Clustering 3K PBMCs with Seurat

Author

Kevin Rue-Albrecht

Generated: 2026-05-28 11:43:55

Libraries

library(cowplot)
library(DropletUtils)
library(scater)
library(scran)
library(scuttle)

Data

Data download

To create a parallel tutorial to the existing tutorial Clustering 3K PBMCs with Seurat, input data files were downloaded from these Zenodo links:

https://zenodo.org/record/3581213/files/genes.tsv
https://zenodo.org/record/3581213/files/barcodes.tsv
https://zenodo.org/record/3581213/files/matrix.mtx

Create a SingleCellExperiment Object

sce <- DropletUtils::read10xCounts(samples = "data")

A Galaxy tool wrapper exists, DropletUtils Read10x, but produces an rdata file which needs to be manually re-typed to rdata.sce or loom depending on the downstream steps.

Switch to user-friendlier gene identifiers

Setting the rownames of the object to “uniquified gene symbols” makes later plots coloured by gene expression more human-friendly. See scater::plotPCA(sce, colour_by = gene_id)). Note that the real gene symbols remain available in rowData(sce)[["Symbol"]].

rownames(sce) <- make.unique(rowData(sce)[["Symbol"]])

Inspect the SingleCellExperiment object.

print(sce)
class: SingleCellExperiment 
dim: 32738 2700 
metadata(1): Samples
assays(1): counts
rownames(32738): MIR1302-10 FAM138A ... AC002321.2 AC002321.1
rowData names(2): ID Symbol
colnames: NULL
colData names(2): Sample Barcode
reducedDimNames(0):
mainExpName: NULL
altExpNames(0):

New Galaxy tool wrapper needed here, simply to run print(sce) into a txt file for inspection.

Preprocessing

Computation of QC metrics

Identify mitochondrial genes.

mt_gene_ids <- rownames(sce)[grep('^MT-', rowData(sce)[["Symbol"]])]
mt_gene_ids
 [1] "MT-ND1"  "MT-ND2"  "MT-CO1"  "MT-CO2"  "MT-ATP8" "MT-ATP6" "MT-CO3" 
 [8] "MT-ND3"  "MT-ND4L" "MT-ND4"  "MT-ND5"  "MT-ND6"  "MT-CYB"

New Galaxy Tool needed to run the code above. The Single-cell quality control with scater tutorial provides a precomputed file of mitochondrial genes.

Calculate the Proportion of Mitochondrial Reads

gene_subsets <- list()
gene_subsets[["MT"]] <- mt_gene_ids
sce <- scuttle::addPerCellQCMetrics(
    x = sce,
    subsets = gene_subsets
)

A Galaxy Tool wrapper exists, Scater: calculate QC metrics, but requires the expression matrix in tabular format.

Filtering of low-quality cells

Visualise QC Metrics.

plot_list <- list()
plot_list[["detected"]] <- scater::plotColData(
    object = sce,
    y = "detected"
)
plot_list[["sum"]] <- scater::plotColData(
    object = sce,
    y = "sum"
)
plot_list[["subsets_MT_percent"]] <- scater::plotColData(
    object = sce,
    y = "subsets_MT_percent"
)
cowplot::plot_grid(plotlist = plot_list, nrow = 1)

TODO: check for existing wrapper Note: In contrast to Seurat, scater can only plot one QC metric at a time. We can use cowplot to combine them into a single figure.

plot_list <- list()
plot_list[["detected"]] <- scater::plotColData(
    object = sce,
    y = "detected",
    x = "sum"
)
plot_list[["subsets_MT_percent"]] <- scater::plotColData(
    object = sce,
    y = "subsets_MT_percent",
    x = "sum"
)
cowplot::plot_grid(plotlist = plot_list, nrow = 1)

Filter Out Low Quality Cells

keep_cells <- sce$detected >= 200 & sce$detected <= 2500 & sce$subsets_MT_percent <= 5
sce <- sce[keep_cells, ]

TODO: check for existing wrapper

Re-Visualise QC Metrics

plot_list <- list()
plot_list[["detected"]] <- scater::plotColData(
    object = sce,
    y = "detected"
)
plot_list[["sum"]] <- scater::plotColData(
    object = sce,
    y = "sum"
)
plot_list[["subsets_MT_percent"]] <- scater::plotColData(
    object = sce,
    y = "subsets_MT_percent"
)
cowplot::plot_grid(plotlist = plot_list, nrow = 1)

plot_list <- list()
plot_list[["detected"]] <- scater::plotColData(
    object = sce,
    y = "detected",
    x = "sum"
)
plot_list[["subsets_MT_percent"]] <- scater::plotColData(
    object = sce,
    y = "subsets_MT_percent",
    x = "sum"
)
cowplot::plot_grid(plotlist = plot_list, nrow = 1)

TODO: check for existing wrapper

Further Preprocessing

Separate Preprocessing Steps

sce <- scuttle::logNormCounts(x = sce)
allf <- scran::modelGeneVar(x = sce)
hvgs <- scran::getTopHVGs(allf, n = 2000)

TODO: check for existing Galaxy tool wrapper. The wrapper should output both: - the table of statistics for later plotting (tsv) - the identifiers of highly variable genes (txt)

message("No direct equivalent to Seurat ScaleData")
No direct equivalent to Seurat ScaleData

TODO: check when this might have an impact on the rest of the tutorial

plot(allf$mean, allf$total)
curve(metadata(allf)$trend(x), add=TRUE, col="dodgerblue")

TODO: check for existing Galaxy tool wrapper. Note that there is no packaged function available for this. Both the help page and the OSCA book give base R code for quick inspection. A Galaxy Tool wrapper could easily produce a nicer ggplot2 graphics including labels for the top N genes as in the Seurat tutorial.

Dimensionality Reduction

Principal Component Analysis

Perform the PCA

sce <- scater::runPCA(
    x = sce,
    ncomponents = 50,
    subset_row = hvgs
)

TODO: check for existing Galaxy tool wrapper.

Visualise the PCA Results

Visualise the PCA Results - Dimensional Loadings

message("No direct equivalent to Seurat VizDimLoadings")
No direct equivalent to Seurat VizDimLoadings

TODO: check when this might have an impact on the rest of the tutorial

Visualise the PCA Results - DimPlot

scater::plotPCA(sce)

TODO: check for existing Galaxy tool wrapper.

Visualise the PCA Results - Feature Plots

scater::plotPCA(sce, colour_by = "CST3")

Note: this relies on the “uniquified gene symbol” rather than the actual gene symbol (available in rowData(sce)[["Symbol"]]). This ensures that every single feature in the dataset can be plotted whether or not it has a unique gene symbol or not.
